events.title: Pedro Álvares Cabral Lands in Brazil
events.detail: On April 22, 1500, a Portuguese fleet of 13 ships commanded by Pedro Álvares Cabral made landfall near present-day Porto Seguro, Bahia. Cabral claimed the territory for the Portuguese Crown, calling it 'Ilha de Vera Cruz.' The indigenous population — estimated at 1 to 7 million — had inhabited the territory for millennia. The landing inaugurated five centuries of Portuguese colonial rule, forced labor, and epidemic disease that devastated indigenous societies.

events.title: Salvador Founded as First Colonial Capital
events.detail: Governor-General Tomé de Sousa founded Salvador (São Salvador da Bahia de Todos os Santos) on May 29, 1549 as the seat of colonial government. The city became the hub of the Atlantic slave trade and sugar economy. An estimated 1.5 million enslaved Africans were disembarked at Salvador over the colonial period, more than any other port in the Americas. The city remained Brazil's capital until 1763.

events.title: Destruction of Quilombo dos Palmares
events.detail: Quilombo dos Palmares, the largest maroon community in the Americas, was destroyed by Portuguese-colonial forces on February 6, 1695. Founded around 1605 in present-day Alagoas, it sheltered an estimated 20,000 to 30,000 escaped enslaved people at its peak. Its leader Zumbi dos Palmares was captured and executed on November 20, 1695. This date is now commemorated as the National Day of Black Consciousness in Brazil.

events.title: Colonial Capital Transferred to Rio de Janeiro
events.detail: The colonial capital was officially transferred from Salvador, Bahia to Rio de Janeiro in 1763, reflecting the southward shift in Brazil's economic center of gravity driven by Minas Gerais gold discoveries. Rio would remain Brazil's capital for over 200 years, until Brasília's inauguration in 1960.

events.title: Inconfidência Mineira — First Independence Plot
events.detail: A conspiracy of elites and intellectuals in Minas Gerais, inspired by American and French revolutions, planned an uprising against Portuguese rule in 1789. Betrayed before it launched, the conspirators were arrested. Joaquim José da Silva Xavier ('Tiradentes'), a dentist and junior officer, was executed by hanging and quartering on April 21, 1792 — the only conspirator to face capital punishment. Tiradentes became the preeminent national martyr; April 21 is a national holiday.

events.title: Portuguese Royal Court Flees to Rio de Janeiro
events.detail: Facing Napoleon's invasion of Portugal, the entire Portuguese royal court sailed to Rio de Janeiro under British naval escort, arriving on March 7, 1808. Prince Regent Dom João VI opened Brazil's ports to friendly nations, ending the colonial trade monopoly. Rio was transformed into a European-style capital with institutions, academies, and printing presses. This unprecedented transfer of a European monarchy to the Americas accelerated Brazil's eventual independence.

events.title: Brazil Elevated to United Kingdom of Portugal
events.detail: On December 16, 1815, Dom João VI elevated Brazil from a colony to an equal partner kingdom, creating the 'United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves.' This was a unique event in colonial history — a colony formally co-equal to the mother country — and laid constitutional groundwork for the eventual separation from Portugal.

events.title: Declaration of Independence — Grito do Ipiranga
events.detail: On September 7, 1822, Crown Prince Dom Pedro I declared Brazil's independence from Portugal on the banks of the Ipiranga River near São Paulo with the legendary cry 'Independência ou Morte!' Brazil became an independent constitutional monarchy — the only monarchy in the Americas — avoiding the civil wars that fractured Spanish colonial possessions. Dom Pedro I was crowned Emperor on December 1, 1822.

events.title: Eusébio de Queirós Law Bans Atlantic Slave Trade
events.detail: Under heavy British pressure, Brazil enacted the Eusébio de Queirós Law in September 1850, formally ending the trans-Atlantic slave trade. However, the internal slave trade within Brazil and the existing enslaved population remained legal for another 38 years. Brazil had received an estimated 4.9 million enslaved Africans — nearly 40% of all those transported across the Atlantic.

events.title: War of the Triple Alliance (Paraguayan War) Begins
events.detail: The deadliest war in South American history began in November 1864 when Paraguay invaded Brazil's Mato Grosso province. Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay formed a Triple Alliance against Paraguay. The conflict lasted until 1870 and decimated Paraguay, killing approximately 60–70% of its population. Brazil suffered over 100,000 military dead. Emperor Pedro II's leadership during the war paradoxically fueled republican sentiment within the officer corps.

events.title: Lei Áurea — Abolition of Slavery
events.detail: On May 13, 1888, Princess Imperial Regent Isabel signed the Lei Áurea (Golden Law), abolishing slavery in Brazil without compensation to slaveholders. Brazil was the last country in the Western Hemisphere to abolish slavery. At the time of abolition, approximately 700,000 people remained enslaved. The act alienated the planter class from the monarchy, contributing directly to the 1889 republican coup.

events.title: Proclamation of the Republic
events.detail: On November 15, 1889, a military coup led by Marshal Deodoro da Fonseca deposed Emperor Dom Pedro II and proclaimed Brazil a republic. The coup was bloodless and largely welcomed by the coffee-growing elite and republican military officers. Pedro II accepted his deposition peacefully and departed for exile in Europe.

events.title: Old Republic: Oligarchic 'Café com Leite' Politics
events.detail: The First Brazilian Republic (1889–1930) was characterized by 'coronelismo' — political bosses controlling rural votes — and the 'política dos governadores,' a spoils system alternating the presidency between São Paulo coffee barons and Minas Gerais cattle ranchers. The system effectively disenfranchised industrial workers, urban poor, and the growing middle class. Direct democracy was formalistic: the landed elite controlled electoral outcomes through fraud and violence.

events.title: Tenentismo — Young Officers' Revolt
events.detail: On July 5, 1922, a group of young military officers revolted at Fort Copacabana in Rio de Janeiro against the oligarchic republic. The 'Eighteen of Copacabana Fort' marched defiantly down the beach against government troops — most were killed or arrested. This revolt launched a cycle of tenentista uprisings (1922–1932) that would contribute to Vargas's rise to power. The tenentes advocated nationalism, industrialization, and social reform.

events.title: Vargas Revolution Ends the Old Republic
events.detail: On October 3, 1930, a military-backed revolution led by Getúlio Vargas overthrew President Washington Luís after the government tried to impose a successor outside the café com leite arrangement. Vargas entered Rio de Janeiro on horseback on October 24. He would govern Brazil for the next 24 years in various capacities, modernizing the state, building nationalist industries, and pioneering Brazilian populism.

events.title: Constitutionalist Revolution — São Paulo Uprising
events.detail: São Paulo's elite launched an armed uprising on July 9, 1932 demanding a constitutional government. The conflict lasted 87 days before federal forces defeated the São Paulo rebels. Though militarily defeated, the revolt succeeded politically: Vargas agreed to draft a new constitution, promulgated in 1934. July 9 remains a public holiday in São Paulo state.

sources.name: Arquivo do Estado de São Paulo

events.title: Estado Novo — Vargas Declares Authoritarian State
events.detail: On November 10, 1937, Vargas dissolved Congress, annulled the 1934 constitution, and proclaimed the 'Estado Novo' (New State), a fascist-influenced authoritarian regime. He suspended elections, banned political parties, censored the press, and consolidated federal power. He simultaneously built Brazil's industrial base — founding the Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional and Petrobras — while suppressing labor opposition with political police (DOPS).

events.title: Military Ousts Vargas, Democracy Restored
events.detail: On October 29, 1945, the Brazilian military deposed Vargas — pressured by the contradiction of Brazil fighting fascism in WWII while governed by a fascist-leaning Estado Novo. General elections were held; Gen. Eurico Dutra was elected president. Vargas returned as an elected senator and ran again for president successfully in 1950.

events.title: Vargas Suicide — 'I Leave Life to Enter History'
events.detail: Under intense military and political pressure demanding his resignation, Getúlio Vargas shot himself in the heart on August 24, 1954. His suicide note, 'Carta Testamento,' accused international economic forces of undermining Brazil's workers. The dramatic death transformed Vargas into a martyr of Brazilian nationalism and triggered mass mourning across the country. His legacy shaped Brazilian politics for generations.

events.title: Kubitschek's '50 Years in 5' Development Plan
events.detail: President Juscelino Kubitschek took office in January 1956 with the slogan 'fifty years of progress in five.' His Programa de Metas targeted industry, energy, transport, and food production. He welcomed foreign investment (Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz), built thousands of kilometers of roads, and presided over an economic boom. GDP grew approximately 7% annually 1956–1960. The cost was accelerating inflation and foreign debt.

events.title: Brasília Inaugurated as New Capital
events.detail: On April 21, 1960, President Kubitschek inaugurated Brasília as Brazil's new capital, fulfilling a constitutional mandate dating to 1891. Designed by Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer, Brasília was built in 41 months in the central plateau. The relocation from Rio de Janeiro was intended to integrate Brazil's interior and symbolize modernist national ambition. Brasília was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987.

events.title: Goulart's Base Reforms Alarm Military
events.detail: President João 'Jango' Goulart proposed sweeping 'Reformas de Base' including land reform, nationalization of oil refineries, and suffrage extension to illiterates. A massive rally of 300,000 in Rio's Praça da Central do Brasil on March 13, 1964 alarmed the military, the Catholic Church, and the United States, which feared a communist turn. This directly triggered the 1964 military coup.

events.title: Military Coup Overthrows Goulart
events.detail: On March 31 – April 1, 1964, Brazilian military forces overthrew President João Goulart, who fled to Uruguay without resistance. The coup was tacitly supported by the United States (Ambassador Lincoln Gordon had authorized Operation Brother Sam naval standby). The military installed Gen. Humberto Castelo Branco and issued the first Institutional Act (AI-1), purging hundreds of political figures and civil servants.

sources.name: Comissão Nacional da Verdade — Relatório Final 2014

events.title: Institutional Act No. 5 (AI-5) — Hardline Repression
events.detail: On December 13, 1968, General-President Artur Costa e Silva signed Institutional Act No. 5 (AI-5), the harshest measure of the dictatorship. AI-5 closed Congress, suspended habeas corpus, empowered the executive to revoke mandates and civil rights, permitted indefinite preventive detention, and enabled total media censorship. The period following AI-5 saw the worst political violence of the regime: systematic torture, disappearances, and killings.

sources.name: Comissão Nacional da Verdade — Relatório Final 2014

events.title: Brazilian 'Economic Miracle' Peaks
events.detail: Under Finance Minister Delfim Netto, Brazil achieved extraordinary growth: GDP expanded 11.1% in 1970, 11.3% in 1971, and 12.1% in 1972. The miracle was built on massive external debt, infrastructure megaprojects (Itaipu dam, Trans-Amazon Highway), and suppression of labor demands. Income concentration worsened: the Gini coefficient rose sharply. The miracle collapsed after the 1973 oil shock, followed by a decade of stagflation.

events.title: Journalist Herzog Murdered in Detention
events.detail: On October 25, 1975, TV Cultura journalist Vladimir Herzog was summoned by military intelligence (DOI-CODI) in São Paulo and found dead the next day. The regime claimed suicide by hanging; evidence of torture was overwhelming. Herzog's death triggered mass protests and an ecumenical memorial by Cardinal Arns — a turning point in civil society resistance. In 2012, a federal judge ruled his death a homicide; the Brazilian state officially acknowledged responsibility.

sources.name: Comissão Nacional da Verdade — Relatório Final 2014

events.title: Lei de Anistia — Amnesty and Abertura
events.detail: On August 28, 1979, President Figueiredo signed the Lei de Anistia, allowing political exiles to return and releasing political prisoners. However, the law also granted effective amnesty to military and police officers responsible for torture and disappearances. Brazil's STF upheld the law in 2010, preventing accountability prosecutions. Human rights organizations continue to demand revision of this self-amnesty.

sources.name: Comissão Nacional da Verdade — Relatório Final 2014

events.title: Diretas Já — Mass Campaign for Direct Elections
events.detail: Beginning January 25, 1984, a nationwide mass movement — 'Diretas Já' (Direct Elections Now) — swept Brazil. Millions took to the streets in the largest political mobilizations in Brazilian history, demanding direct presidential elections. The constitutional amendment failed in Congress on April 25, 1984. Nevertheless, the movement accelerated the military's managed transition to civilian rule, culminating in Tancredo Neves's election in January 1985.

events.title: Tancredo Neves Elected — Dies Before Inauguration
events.detail: On January 15, 1985, Tancredo Neves was elected president by the Electoral College — the first civilian president after 21 years of military rule. He fell gravely ill before his March 15 inauguration and died on April 21, 1985, never having taken office. His vice president, José Sarney — a former supporter of the military regime — became Brazil's first civilian president, serving until 1990.

events.title: Constituição Cidadã Promulgated
events.detail: On October 5, 1988, Brazil promulgated its current constitution, known as the 'Constituição Cidadã' (Citizen's Constitution). The most progressive in Brazilian history, it enshrined social rights (health, education, housing), environmental protections, indigenous land rights, and broad civil liberties. It created the SUS public health system, mandated indigenous land demarcation, and established the Ministério Público as an independent institution.

events.title: President Collor Impeached for Corruption
events.detail: Fernando Collor de Mello, Brazil's first directly elected president (1989), was impeached by the Chamber of Deputies on September 29, 1992 on corruption charges. Millions of 'caras-pintadas' protesters took to the streets. Collor resigned on December 29, 1992, just before Senate conviction, but was barred from politics for 8 years. Vice President Itamar Franco completed the term, appointing Fernando Henrique Cardoso as Finance Minister.

events.title: Plano Real Defeats Hyperinflation
events.detail: On July 1, 1994, Finance Minister Fernando Henrique Cardoso launched the Plano Real, replacing the cruzeiro real with the new 'real' currency at parity with the US dollar. The plan broke the hyperinflationary inertia that had plagued Brazil for decades. Annual inflation fell from 2,477% (1993) to 916% (1994) to just 22% (1995) and single digits by 1997. The success propelled FHC to a landslide presidential victory in October 1994.

events.title: Real Devaluation and Currency Crisis
events.detail: In January 1999, Brazil abandoned the dollar peg under pressure from capital outflows following Russia's 1998 default. The real lost nearly 50% of its value within weeks. An IMF bailout of $41.5 billion was arranged. The crisis forced structural fiscal adjustment but the economy recovered; the real stabilized. Brazil avoided the sovereign default that hit Argentina in 2001.

events.title: Lula Elected President on Fourth Attempt
events.detail: On October 27, 2002, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva of the Workers' Party (PT) won Brazil's presidential election with 61.3% of the vote. Lula — a former metalworkers' union leader and political prisoner during the dictatorship — represented the first time the Brazilian left won the presidency. To calm financial markets, Lula published a 'Letter to the Brazilian People' promising to honor existing contracts and debt obligations.

events.title: Bolsa Família — Flagship Cash Transfer Program
events.detail: On October 20, 2003, President Lula launched Bolsa Família, consolidating existing social transfer programs into a single conditional cash transfer targeting families in poverty. By 2010, the program reached 12.7 million families. Combined with economic growth, Bolsa Família helped lift 28 million Brazilians out of poverty and reduced Brazil's Gini coefficient from 0.59 (2001) to 0.53 (2011). It became the most studied social program in the developing world.

events.title: Mensalão — Vote-Buying Scandal Rocks Lula Government
events.detail: In June 2005, investigation revealed a 'mensalão' (big monthly payment) scheme: PT congressional leaders allegedly paid allied legislators monthly stipends to vote with the government. The scandal led to resignations of senior PT figures including chief of staff José Dirceu. STF convicted 25 defendants in 2012. Lula was re-elected in 2006 despite the scandal.

events.title: Pre-Salt Oil Discovery and Commodity Boom
events.detail: In November 2007, Petrobras announced the discovery of the Tupi/Lula pre-salt oil field, estimated at 5–8 billion barrels — one of the world's largest deepwater discoveries. Brazil's economy boomed 2007–2010, driven by commodity exports to China, rising wages, and credit expansion. GDP growth hit 7.5% in 2010. Brazil joined the G20, expanded BRICS participation, and won hosting rights for the 2014 World Cup and 2016 Olympics. Lula left office with a 90% approval rating.

events.title: Dilma Rousseff Elected — First Female President
events.detail: On October 31, 2010, Dilma Rousseff, Lula's handpicked successor and a former guerrilla who was tortured during the military dictatorship, was elected Brazil's first female president with 56.1% of the vote. She was re-elected in October 2014 against Aécio Neves in a deeply polarizing race decided by 3.3 million votes. Her second term was immediately engulfed in recession, the Lava Jato investigation, and political mobilization to remove her.

events.title: Operation Lava Jato — Largest Corruption Investigation
events.detail: On March 17, 2014, Federal Police in Curitiba launched Operation Lava Jato (Car Wash), initially targeting money laundering. Investigations rapidly exposed a systematic corruption scheme at Petrobras: construction companies paid bribes (3–5% of contracts) to Petrobras directors, who funneled money to political parties. The investigation recovered over R$4 billion, convicted more than 100 executives and politicians, and fundamentally altered Brazilian democracy. Judge Sérgio Moro became a national figure.

sources.name: Ministério Público Federal — Operação Lava Jato

events.title: Dilma Rousseff Impeached
events.detail: On August 31, 2016, the Brazilian Senate voted 61–20 to remove President Dilma Rousseff, completing the impeachment process begun by the Chamber in April. The formal charge was 'pedaladas fiscais' — unauthorized use of public banks to cover government expenditures and mask budget deficits. Dilma and supporters called it a 'parliamentary coup.' Vice President Michel Temer assumed the presidency. The STF did not find criminal elements in the conduct.

events.title: Lula Convicted and Jailed by Moro
events.detail: On July 12, 2017, Judge Sérgio Moro convicted Lula of corruption related to a Guarujá apartment allegedly given to him by contractor OAS. Lula began serving a 12-year sentence on April 7, 2018. In 2021, STF Justice Edson Fachin annulled all Lava Jato convictions of Lula, ruling the Curitiba court lacked jurisdiction. Leaked messages ('Vaza Jato') had revealed Moro coordinating improperly with prosecutors against Lula.

events.title: Jair Bolsonaro Elected President
events.detail: On October 28, 2018, Jair Bolsonaro of the far-right PSL won Brazil's presidential election with 55.1% of the vote against PT's Fernando Haddad, in an election dominated by anti-PT sentiment, WhatsApp disinformation, and security concerns. His environment minister dismantled regulatory agencies, and Amazon deforestation surged to 11,568 km² in 2019 — a 12-year high.

events.title: COVID-19: Bolsonaro's Denial and 700,000 Deaths
events.detail: Brazil recorded over 700,000 COVID-19 deaths — the second highest death toll globally. President Bolsonaro consistently downplayed the pandemic, calling it 'a little flu,' promoted unproven treatments, fired two health ministers who advocated lockdowns, and resisted vaccine purchases. A Senate inquiry (CPI da COVID, 2021) concluded Bolsonaro bore criminal responsibility for negligence. Brazil's vaccination campaign ultimately accelerated in late 2021, achieving high coverage.

events.title: Lula Defeats Bolsonaro in Narrow Runoff
events.detail: On October 30, 2022, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva defeated incumbent Jair Bolsonaro with 50.9% vs 49.1% of the vote — the narrowest margin since 1994. Bolsonaro never formally conceded; his allies blockaded highways nationwide for weeks. The TSE and military certified the result. Bolsonaro flew to Florida before the inauguration and stayed abroad for months, subsequently facing charges for alleged coup plotting.

events.title: January 8 — Bolsonaristas Storm Brasília Institutions
events.detail: On January 8, 2023, one week after Lula's inauguration, thousands of Bolsonaro supporters stormed and ransacked the Presidential Palace, Congress, and the Supreme Court in Brasília. Rioters smashed artworks, destroyed furniture, and called for military intervention. Over 1,430 people were arrested; by 2024, 310 had been convicted with sentences averaging 15–17 years. STF Justice Alexandre de Moraes investigated Bolsonaro for alleged coordination with the rioters. Property damage exceeded $14 million USD.

events.title: Amazon Deforestation Hits 9-Year Low Under Lula
events.detail: Brazil's Amazon deforestation fell 30.6% in 2024 to 6,288 km² — the lowest level in nine years, according to INPE data released in November 2024. President Lula's government reactivated environmental agencies dismantled under Bolsonaro and pledged to end deforestation by 2030. However, fire-related forest loss surged to 2.78 million hectares during a historic drought, highlighting the complex interplay between deforestation policy and climate change.
